pattern_recognition: This problem falls under the category of substring problems, specifically focusing on uniqueness within a string. The signals include the need to track characters and their positions, as well as the requirement to maximize length without repetition.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Generate all possible substrings and check for uniqueness.
approaches.algorithm: Iterate through all substrings, using a set to track characters and determine if they are unique, keeping track of the maximum length found.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Use a sliding window technique to maintain a dynamic range of characters while iterating through the string.
approaches.algorithm: Utilize two pointers to represent the current substring and a hash map to track the last seen index of characters, adjusting the start pointer as needed to ensure uniqueness.

dry_run: For the input 'abcabcbb', start with an empty set and two pointers. Move the end pointer to include characters until a repeat is found. When 'b' is encountered again, move the start pointer to the right of the first 'b', continuing until the end of the string. The maximum length recorded is 3 for the substring 'abc'.
edge_cases: Consider an empty string which should return 0. A single character string should return 1. Strings with all identical characters should return 1. Large inputs should be handled efficiently without exceeding memory limits.
common_pitfalls: Common mistakes include: 1) Not resetting the start pointer correctly when a duplicate is found, leading to incorrect substring lengths. 2) Failing to account for the last substring after the loop ends, missing the maximum length. 3) Using a static data structure that does not allow for dynamic updates, causing incorrect tracking of character positions.
